summary: The current build evidence for the GameConfigStore fullscreen-optimization tuple now covers the observed 25H2 story: Microsoft documents the feature area, the Win25H2Clean reversible probe confirmed the tuple before restoring the baseline, svchost.exe backed by resourcepolicyserver.dll queried GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible live in Procmon, and our Ghidra export tied the same build back to the GameConfigStore RPC server code path.

setting.name: Fullscreen Optimizations
setting.casual_explanation: Fullscreen Optimizations is a Windows feature that tries to blend fullscreen-style game performance with some modern desktop and presentation behavior. People usually turn it off only when a specific game behaves badly with it.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it when they are troubleshooting stutter, unstable frame pacing, odd alt-tab behavior, capture conflicts, or game-specific compatibility problems.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Turning Fullscreen Optimizations off can help a stubborn game, but it can also remove the newer presentation behavior Windows is trying to provide. The feature is real, but the raw GameConfigStore tuple still is not published by Microsoft as a supported registry contract.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents Fullscreen Optimizations as a real Windows feature and exposes supported user-facing ways to disable it through compatibility UI. The current app writes HKCU\System\GameConfigStore values GameDVR_FSEBehavior = 2, GameDVR_FSEBehaviorMode = 2, GameDVR_HonorUserFSEBehaviorMode = 1, and GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible = 1. The Win25H2Clean reversible probe confirmed missing -> 2/2/1/1 -> missing on the same path. A 2026-03-26 Procmon pass then captured svchost.exe querying HKCU\System\GameConfigStore\GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible with Data: 1 while Gaming and Advanced Graphics triggers were active. On the same date, our Ghidra export on ResourcePolicyServer.dll tied the current build back to the GameConfigStore RPC server code path and the same GameDVR_* string set. That gives this record current-build runtime and code-side proof, but it still stays research-gated because Microsoft does not publish the raw GameConfigStore tuple as a supported registry contract and the live capture did not read every tuple member in one pass.

allowed_values.label: Observed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: The current Win25H2Clean baseline leaves the GameConfigStore fullscreen-optimization tuple unset.

allowed_values.label: Observed app FSO-off profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app treats these four values as the disable-FSO profile, and the reversible VM probe confirmed that tuple on the same path.

targets.notes: Tracked as an observed user-side GameConfigStore mapping only.

app_current_implementation.notes: Guest-side reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the current GameConfigStore write tuple and restore cycle.

windows_defaults.label: Observed baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Current builds where the GameConfigStore fullscreen-optimization tuple is left unset

states.rationale: Leave the current GameConfigStore tuple unset.

windows_defaults.notes: This is the current observed baseline from the Win25H2Clean reversible probe, not a primary Microsoft registry contract.

recommended_profiles.label: Observed default

states.rationale: Use the current observed missing baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the current observed GameConfigStore baseline.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to force the current disable-FSO tuple for troubleshooting.
tradeoffs: Leaves the current user-side compatibility tuple alone.
Still stays research-gated because the raw registry contract is not published by Microsoft.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile

states.rationale: Observed app write used as the current disable-FSO implementation.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the current app's observed disable-FSO tuple for compatibility testing.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want only primary-doc-backed registry contracts.
tradeoffs: The tuple is backed by a reversible VM proof plus current-build Procmon and Ghidra evidence.
The exact GameConfigStore semantics are still not published by Microsoft as a raw registry contract.
recommended_profiles.notes: The feature is real, but the raw tuple still stays research-gated.

evidence.title: Microsoft Support: Optimizations for windowed games in Windows
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the Fullscreen Optimizations and related presentation feature area from the user perspective, confirming that this is a real Windows feature even though this page does not publish the raw GameConfigStore registry values.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for Fullscreen Optimizations
evidence.summary: The repo's research notes list the four GameConfigStore values and attribute their semantics to values found in ResourcePolicyServer.dll, which is useful provenance but not a primary Microsoft registry publication.

evidence.title: Procmon capture - svchost.exe GameConfigStore fullscreen tuple read
evidence.summary: A Win25H2Clean Procmon pass around Gaming and Advanced Graphics triggers captured svchost.exe querying HKCU\System\GameConfigStore\GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible with Data: 1 while the current tuple was applied.

evidence.title: Our Ghidra decompilation - ResourcePolicyServer GameConfigStore path
evidence.summary: Our 25H2 Ghidra export on ResourcePolicyServer.dll found the GameDVR_FSEBehavior string set, the GameConfigStore RPC server source paths, and registry helpers that open and validate GameConfigStore values on the current build.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes the four GameConfigStore values 2/2/1/1 for the current Fullscreen Optimizations tweak.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean reversible probe - Fullscreen optimizations override bundle
evidence.summary: Guest reversible probe toggled the GameConfigStore fullscreen-optimization bundle values and restored the original missing state, confirming the current app write set on Win25H2Clean.

decision.why: Microsoft's feature-level doc, the reversible VM probe, the svchost.exe Procmon read on the current build, and the ResourcePolicyServer.dll Ghidra export all line up on the same GameConfigStore surface. That is enough to track the missing baseline and the 2/2/1/1 tuple as current observed states, but the record stays research-gated because the raw tuple is still undocumented by Microsoft and the live pass did not read every tuple member.

open_questions: Can the remaining tuple members be captured live on the same build through the same ResourcePolicyServer path?
Can the GameConfigStore tuple be tied to a Microsoft-published registry contract rather than only the feature area?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: fullscreen-diag.txt: svchost.exe RegQueryValue HKCU\System\GameConfigStore\GameDVR_DXGIHonorFSEWindowsCompatible Data: 1. resourcepolicysrv-fullscreen-ghidra.md: ResourcePolicyServer.dll carries the GameConfigStore RPC server path and the GameDVR_* string set.
validation_proof.notes: The reversible VM probe still covers missing -> 2/2/1/1 -> missing. The 2026-03-26 Procmon and Ghidra passes add a current-build svchost.exe read on GameConfigStore plus direct ResourcePolicyServer code-side proof.
